item.text:
# AUTHORITATIVE SEMANTIC COMMISSION — CH-01

CH-01, “The Debate Can End,” owns one corrective move: past rules, lapses, and continuing management do not prove that the reader is weak or that sugar is uniquely powerful. They show that behavior was targeted while BAD SUGAR’s status as a special reward remained intact. Treat prior effort as evidence of effort spent on the wrong task, not defective character.

The chapter receives a reader who is skeptical, exhausted by restriction and restarting, or maintaining a workable plan that never stops requiring attention. Its primary facets are the Restriction Veteran (PER-02), Managed-Truce Maintainer (PER-05), and Decision-Tax Escapee (PER-06). The plan-frozen reader-voice line is:

> “I don’t want a perfect streak. I want the debate to stop.” (RD-08; original reader voice, not participant testimony.)

Establish an evidence-honest trust contract. The available authority is reader recognition, transparent distinctions, bounded lived accounts, and permission to question the book. There is no author escape story, clinical pedigree, success rate, population-scale result, or guarantee. First-person guide language may promise honesty and invite inquiry but may not imply personal experience or outcomes.

Briefly distinguish three possible states without presenting them as a universal sequence:

- Suppression: behavior is controlled while the lost-reward belief remains, so denial or a lapse can still feel highly charged.
- Managed truce: boundaries, resets, and daily practices may provide genuine relief while continuing to demand management.
- Desire-level freedom: BAD SUGAR is absent from the active decision catalog—no planned doses, felt deprivation, daily negotiation, or dietary-control identity.

The third state is the book’s promised destination, not an outcome this chapter proves. Moderation remains the reader’s right, and managed truce must not be mocked or renamed failure. “Planned dose” denotes deliberate decision-loop behavior, not a pharmacological event.

Make the decision tax tangible: attention and time spent `anticipating, bargaining, and starting over`. SC-01 is the bounded report by Ryan Andrews that he spent “at least 2 hours each week” debating whether to eat dessert. This is one adult’s reported private cost, not a prevalence estimate. The chapter may use AN-11, the two-tollbooth road, only as a preview: continuing carries a real cost, while stopping appears to carry the cost of losing a valued reward. Do not complete the later argument about how that false stopping cost is created or removed.

The chapter debuts these frozen formulations exactly:

- M-01: `You can test whether BAD SUGAR owns any special or irreplaceable pleasure, without denying pleasant taste or giving up your right to choose.`
- M-02: `without deprivation, without battle, and without a lifetime of rules`
- M-03: `the sugar trap`
- M-06: `anticipating, bargaining, and starting over`

Here, `the sugar trap` names an externalizable learned structure, never an inner creature, attacker, parasite, or independent neurological agent. “Decision tax” means the attention and negotiation consumed by the recurring question. Pleasant taste and calories remain real; this chapter does not yet litigate whether pleasant taste establishes necessity or irreplaceable value.

The governing instruction must appear verbatim:

> **KEEP YOUR CHOICE AND BEGIN WITH RELIEF. Continue eating as you normally do while you read unless qualified clinical care requires something different; do not begin a new restriction campaign for this book.** (I-01)

This is an invitation to investigate, not an instruction to quit. The target is the anticipation, bargaining, reward, rescue, grazing, and restarting loop around the plan’s bounded BAD SUGAR category—not nutrition purity. Do not adjudicate individual foods or supply the formal category definition here; CH-02 owns that line. It is enough to protect whole fruit, ordinary meals, ordinary carbohydrates, incidental traces, and clinically required nutrition from implied inclusion.

Assigned evidence may support only the following work:

- **EV-L04 / S-020:** In a short feasibility intervention concerning indulgent foods and beverages, one participant said, “If I deny myself something it turns up somewhere else and worse.” This is an **EXACT_QUOTE**, S-020#E-001, Results §3.12, participant labelled Female, 58 years. Another participant described doing well for weeks before one lapse became a period in which there was something every day (S-020#E-003, Table 1, “Relapse,” Female, 60 years). These accounts can recognize deprivation belief and lapse-collapse interpretation. They do not establish a general backfire mechanism, prove that practical total freedom fails, or show that one bite causes continued consumption. Attribution: Madigan CD, Hill AJ, Caterson ID, Burk J, Hendy C, Chalkley A, *Nutrients* 15(6), 1389, DOI 10.3390/nu15061389.

- **EV-L06 / S-021:** Nell Kauls described relief from obsessive food thoughts with the metaphor, “I feel like I've been let out of jail.” This is an **EXACT_QUOTE**, S-021#E-001, transcript freedom moment. The same account described daily actions, personal boundaries, difficult occasions, and next-meal or next-day resets. Use the combination to honour genuine relief while identifying continuing management as managed truce rather than desire absence. This is one named self-report in a commercial wellness-program context, not evidence of program efficacy or a general outcome, and it does not show that management is unnecessary for everyone. Attribution: Nell Kauls, interviewed by Brandy Buro, *Dishing Up Nutrition*, Nutritional Weight & Wellness, 4 April 2024.

- **EV-L08 / S-023:** Ryan Andrews reported spending at least two hours weekly debating dessert, then reported twelve months without added sugar through birthdays and major holidays, not missing desserts, and removing desserts from his decision catalog without treating them as metaphysically forbidden. Use this only to illustrate the possible difference between recurring control and decision quiet. It is one author-published self-report; it supplies no efficacy rate, probability, mechanism, universal ease, medical or weight outcome, expected taste change, or general moderation result. Its scope covers desserts and added sugar, not every refined or junk-carbohydrate product within the book’s broader target. Do not front-load the full case narrative reserved for CH-18. Attribution: Ryan Andrews, MS, MA, RD, RYT, CSCS, “Sugar daddy: My year without desserts,” Precision Nutrition.

Keep observation and inference distinct: the sources report what particular people said or did; the chapter may use those reports to make states of control, management, and decision quiet recognizable. They cannot prove why those states occurred, how common they are, or what this book will produce.

Clinical and eating-disorder boundaries govern the invitation. Medical glucose, prescribed nutrition, diabetes or metabolic treatment, pregnancy-related instructions, and eating-disorder care are outside the method; qualified clinical instructions always outrank the book. Offer no calorie target, weight-loss promise, carbohydrate doctrine, medical interpretation, or physiological timeline. If the inquiry intensifies food fear, restriction, ingredient surveillance, or an eating-disorder pattern, self-experimentation is not the answer; qualified care is.

Do not claim that every diet fails, every restriction backfires, or every reader will find freedom easy. Do not diagnose addiction, withdrawal, compulsion, or a brain mechanism. Do not explain the later sweet-reward-script/open-loop mechanism, fully prosecute the deprivation method, resolve both fears represented by the tollbooths, define the complete BAD SUGAR line, invoke commercial engineering, or ask the reader to cross the no-planned-dose threshold. Avoid health fear, shame, willpower language, purity language, and the book’s banned sacrifice or battle framing except when immediately identifying a belief the chapter corrects.

The understanding handed forward is deliberately modest but decisive: the reader’s history is not a character verdict; behavior control and changed valuation are different outcomes; continuing negotiation is a real cost; and it is legitimate to investigate whether a precisely bounded target owns any special benefit at all.
